unit.name: Recap: explicit-density models and GAN setup
unit.description: Connects prior explicit-density material to implicit-density modeling and introduces GANs.
unit.summary: The lecture opens by re-establishing the generative-model taxonomy: discriminative models learn p(y|x), unconditional generative models learn p(x), and conditional generative models learn p(x|y), with the key distinction being what the probability distribution normalizes over [ts=20s]. It reviews explicit-density methods from the prior lecture: autoregressive image models turn pixels or subpixels into a discrete sequence modeled by an RNN or Transformer [ts=100s], while VAEs jointly train an encoder q(z|x) and decoder p(x|z) to maximize a variational lower bound on data likelihood [ts=132s]. The new focus is implicit-density models, which cannot return a numerical p(x) but can sample from the learned distribution [ts=184s]. GANs are introduced as a direct implicit-density method: sample a latent z from a simple prior such as a unit Gaussian, pass it through a generator G(z), and tune the induced generator distribution pG to match pdata [ts=320s]. A discriminator D is added to classify real versus fake images, and the generator learns from discriminator gradients backpropagated through the generated image [ts=455s], [ts=512s].

key_points.text: Discriminative models learn p(y|x), generative models learn p(x), and conditional generative models learn p(x|y).
Implicit density models cannot output a density p(x) but can sample from the learned distribution.
GANs give up directly modeling p(x) but still provide a way to sample from the fitted distribution.
A latent z is sampled from a simple prior p(z), typically a unit Gaussian, then passed through G(z).
GAN training tries to make the generator-induced distribution pG match pdata.
The discriminator D classifies input images as real or fake.
The generator receives learning signal as gradients backpropagated through the discriminator and generated image.

kp_catalog.name: Generative model taxonomy
kp_catalog.description: Distinguishes discriminative p(y|x), unconditional generative p(x), and conditional generative p(x|y) modeling, emphasizing normalization and conditioning.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because generative model taxonomy formalizes how distinguishes discriminative p(y|x), unconditional generative p(x), and conditional generative p(x|y) modeling, emphasizing normalization and conditioning, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Implicit density modeling
kp_catalog.description: Models that cannot compute p(x) explicitly but can still sample from the learned distribution.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because implicit density modeling formalizes how models that cannot compute p(x) explicitly but can still sample from the learned distribution, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Likelihood-based explicit-density recap
kp_catalog.description: Autoregressive models and VAEs are reviewed as maximum-likelihood or lower-bound methods for explicit density modeling.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because likelihood-based explicit-density recap clarifies autoregressive models and VAEs are reviewed as maximum-likelihood or lower-bound methods for explicit density modeling, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: GAN latent generator distribution
kp_catalog.description: Sampling z from a simple prior and mapping through G(z) induces pG, which should match pdata.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because gAN latent generator distribution formalizes how sampling z from a simple prior and mapping through G(z) induces pG, which should match pdata, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: GAN discriminator feedback
kp_catalog.description: The discriminator classifies real/fake samples and supplies gradients that train the generator.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because gAN discriminator feedback clarifies the discriminator classifies real/fake samples and supplies gradients that train the generator, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
